2604.07778.title: The Accountability Horizon: An Impossibility Theorem for Governing Human-Agent Collectives
2604.07778.paper_summary: Existing accountability frameworks for AI systems, legal, ethical, and regulatory, rest on a shared assumption: for any consequential outcome, at least one identifiable person had enough involvement and foresight to bear meaningful responsibility. This paper proves that agentic AI systems violate this assumption not as an engineering limitation but as a mathematical necessity once autonomy exceeds a computable threshold. We introduce Human-Agent Collectives, a formalisation of joint human-AI systems where agents are modelled as state-policy tuples within a shared structural causal model. Autonomy is characterised through a four-dimensional information-theoretic profile (epistemic, executive, evaluative, social); collective behaviour through interaction graphs and joint action spaces. We axiomatise legitimate accountability through four minimal properties: Attributability (responsibility requires causal contribution), Foreseeability Bound (responsibility cannot exceed predictive capacity), Non-Vacuity (at least one agent bears non-trivial responsibility), and Completeness (all responsibility must be fully allocated). Our central result, the Accountability Incompleteness Theorem, proves that for any collective whose compound autonomy exceeds the Accountability Horizon and whose interaction graph contains a human-AI feedback cycle, no framework can satisfy all four properties simultaneously. The impossibility is structural: transparency, audits, and oversight cannot resolve it without reducing autonomy. Below the threshold, legitimate frameworks exist, establishing a sharp phase transition. Experiments on 3,000 synthetic collectives confirm all predictions with zero violations. This is the first impossibility result in AI governance, establishing a formal boundary below which current paradigms remain valid and above which distributed accountability mechanisms become necessary.
2604.07778.paper_summary_zh: 現有的 AI 系統問責框架，包括法律、倫理和監管，基於一個共同的假設：對於任何有重大後果的結果，至少有一個可識別的人足夠參與並具備前瞻性，承擔有意義的責任。本文證明，代理 AI 系統違反了這一假設，這不是工程上的限制，而是當自主性超過可計算的閾值時的數學必然性。我們介紹了人類-代理集體，這是一種聯合人類-AI 系統的形式化，其中代理被建模為共享結構因果模型中的狀態-策略元組。自主性通過四維信息理論特徵（認識、執行、評估、社會）來表徵；集體行為則通過互動圖和聯合行動空間來表徵。我們通過四個最小性質來公理化合法的問責性：可歸屬性（責任需要因果貢獻）、可預見性界限（責任不能超過預測能力）、非空性（至少一個代理承擔非平凡的責任）和完整性（所有責任必須完全分配）。我們的核心結果，即問責不完全性定理，證明了對於任何其複合自主性超過問責地平線且其互動圖包含人類-AI 反饋循環的集體，沒有任何框架能同時滿足所有四個性質。這一不可能性是結構性的：透明度、審計和監督無法解決這一問題，除非降低自主性。在閾值以下，存在合法的框架，建立了一個明確的相變。對 3,000 個合成集體的實驗確認了所有預測，且沒有違規。這是 AI 治理中的第一個不可能性結果，確立了一個正式的邊界，在此邊界以下當前的範式仍然有效，而在此邊界以上分散的問責機制變得必要。
2604.07778.author: Haileleol Tibebu et.al.
